topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon light seemed to cling to the dusty windowpanes, thick and slow. Every movement felt like wading through warm honey. I hadn't managed to lift my head from the textbook for nearly an hour, the words blurring into meaningless shapes on the page. A heavy, leaden feeling settled in my chest, making each breath a deliberate, laborious effort. I sighed, a long, drawn-out expulsion of air that seemed to carry the weight of the entire afternoon. Then, a knock at the door. It was him. Mark. My childhood tormentor. I braced myself, my shoulders hunching instinctively, the familiar tightening in my throat starting. When I opened the door, he stood there, impeccably dressed, a calm, almost unnerving composure about him. "Hey," he said, his voice smooth, devoid of any of the sharp edges I remembered. "I just wanted to check in. You seem a little… adrift today."

[story 2]

My limbs felt weighted, as if gravity had suddenly doubled its pull. I stared at the lukewarm coffee in my mug, the brown liquid refusing to inspire any kind of energy. My eyelids felt glued shut, making the world outside the window a distant, muted watercolor. I kept shifting in my chair, a subtle, restless twitch that betrayed the deep inertia gripping me. The silence in the apartment was oppressive, amplifying the dull thrumming in my ears. Then, the doorbell rang. I hesitated, my hand hovering over the cool metal of the buzzer. It was him. Mark. The memory of his sneers, the way he used to dismantle my confidence brick by painful brick, flashed behind my eyes, momentarily disrupting the fog. When I finally answered, my voice came out thin and reedy. "What do you want?"

[story 3]

I moved through the motions of the day like a marionette whose strings had been tangled. Each step required conscious effort, a slow negotiation with my own tired muscles. My mind felt like a thick, viscous fluid, resistant to any sharp thought or quick reaction. I kept rubbing my temples, a futile attempt to clear the internal static that made focusing impossible. The sheer effort of existing felt monumental. Suddenly, a familiar shadow fell across my doorway. It was Mark. He wasn't the same boy who used to corner me by the lockers; he possessed a quiet gravity now, an almost scholarly air. "You look spent," he observed, his tone gentle, probing. "Is everything alright? You seem completely drained."
